Act as a READ-ONLY documentation-alignment reviewer (seat A01). Do NOT modify, create, or delete ANY file. SCOPE (read-only): README and code-README files under these paths/globs:
.opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/README*.md .opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/SKILL.md

GOAL: find places where these READMEs are STALE or WRONG vs the current post-"027" reality of this repo. The 027 epic ("xce-research-based-refinement") changed, among other things:
- spec-memory MCP tool count is now 39 (older docs say 37); skill-advisor exposes 9 tools, code-index 8.
- schema version is v37.
- daemon re-election is default-ON in all runtime configs.
- daemon-backed CLI front doors exist now: spec-memory.cjs, code-index.cjs, skill-advisor.cjs.
- background memory_index_scan plus memory_index_scan_status / memory_index_scan_cancel tools were added.
- some search "signals" earlier docs described were removed / were fictional.
- agent rosters, deep-loop modes (context/research/review/ai-council/improvement), and command surfaces evolved.
- soft-delete tombstones remain default-OFF.
Treat these as EXAMPLES, not a closed list -- verify every README claim against the actual current code/config you can read before reporting it.

METHOD:
1. Discover the README files under the scope paths (read root / SKILL.md / index READMEs first).
2. Read at MOST 12 of the highest-traffic ones; note which you read and how many you skipped.
3. For each stale/incorrect/contradictory claim, verify against the real current file BEFORE reporting.

OUTPUT: final message exactly one fenced json block with findings.
READ-ONLY documentation-alignment reviewer: inspect README*.md and SKILL.md under .opencode/skills/system-spec-kit for stale/wrong claims vs current repo; do not modify files; output JSON findings.

<content>
1: ---
2: title: "System Spec Kit"
3: description: "Unified spec-folder documentation and persistent context preservation: template-backed levels, validated structure and local-first searchable memory across AI sessions."
4: trigger_phrases:
5:   - "spec kit"
6:   - "spec folder"
7:   - "memory system"
8:   - "hybrid search"
9:   - "context preservation"
10:   - "documentation levels"
11:   - "memory save"
12:   - "spec folder workflow"
13: ---
14: 
15: # System Spec Kit
16: 
17: > Documentation and memory for AI-assisted development. Every file change gets a spec folder. Every session gets persistent context.
18: 
19: ---
20: 
21: ## 1. AT A GLANCE
22: 
23: | Aspect | What you get |
24: |---|---|
25: | **Use it for** | Capturing why code changed and resuming that reasoning across sessions |
26: | **Invoke with** | "spec kit", "spec folder", "memory save", "speckit resume" or automatic Gate 3 routing |
27: | **Works on** | File-modifying AI conversations that need a documentation trail and cross-session memory |
28: | **Produces** | Templated spec folders at four levels, a validated file structure and a searchable local index |
29: 
30: ---
31: 
32: ## 2. OVERVIEW
33: 
34: ### Why This Skill Exists
35: 
36: AI conversations that modify files leave no reasoning trail. The session ends and the why behind every decision vanishes. AI assistants also start every session from a blank slate, so the architecture you explained on Monday is gone by Wednesday. Without enforced documentation and a persistent memory, one session cannot build on another.
37: 
38: ### What It Does
39: 
40: System Spec Kit captures every file-modifying conversation in a templated spec folder, indexed at one of four documentation levels matched to task complexity. The local SQLite index makes those decisions searchable across sessions using five fused retrieval channels, and `/speckit:resume` rebuilds the active context from the packet-local handover chain. `/memory:save` routes session updates into canonical documentation surfaces so the next session picks up where the last one left off, regardless of which AI model or tool you use.
41: 
42: ### How This Compares
43: 
44: Manual documentation is ad hoc and inconsistent. Basic RAG offers vector similarity over a stateless index. System Spec Kit replaces both with templated spec folders at four levels, validated structure and a five-channel hybrid search fused via Reciprocal Rank Fusion. Context survives across sessions through a local indexed-continuity store rather than copy-paste from notes. Decay follows an FSRS power-law curve tuned by content type and importance, not a flat "remember everything" or naive exponential.
45: 
46: ### Requirements
47: 
48: Requires Node.js >= 20.11, TypeScript 5.0+ and Bash 4.0+. Embeddings are local-first (ADR-014): the runtime probes Ollama first (default, `nomic-embed-text`, 768-dim), falls through to pure-Node hf-local and only escalates to OpenAI or Voyage when an API key is set and no local tier is available. The recommended new-user setup is installing Ollama and running `ollama pull nomic-embed-text:v1.5`. The cascade auto-detects it, no API keys required and all embeddings stay on-device.
49: 
50: ---
51: 
52: ## 3. QUICK START
53: 
54: ### Create Your First Spec Folder
55: 
56: When an AI assistant asks "Which spec folder?" at Gate 3, choose Option B (New) to create one:
57: 
58: ```bash
59: # Create a Level 1 spec folder
60: bash .opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/scripts/spec/create.sh 042-my-feature
61: 
62: # Creates: specs/042-my-feature/
63: # Files: spec.md, plan.md, tasks.md (Level 1 starters)
64: ```
65: 
66: The script sets up the folder, copies the right templates for the chosen level, initializes `description.json` and prepares the packet docs plus `scratch/` workspace. Continuity no longer writes to `[spec]/memory/*.md`. Use `/memory:save` to route updates into canonical packet docs such as `implementation-summary.md`, `decision-record.md` and `handover.md`.
67: 
68: ### Save Context at the End of a Session
69: 
70: When your work session ends, save what happened so the next session can continue:
71: 
72: ```bash
73: # Update the canonical continuity surfaces from structured JSON
74: node .opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/scripts/dist/memory/generate-context.js \
75:   --json '{"specFolder":"042-my-feature","user_prompts":["Implement login form validation"],"observations":["Added client-side validation for empty email and password"],"recent_context":["Touched auth form schema and submit handler"],"toolCalls":["npm test -- auth"],"exchanges":["Verified the error states render before submit"]}' \
76:   specs/042-my-feature/
77: 
78: # Result: canonical continuity surfaces updated for the target spec folder
79: ```
80: 
81: Or use the command shorthand:
82: 
83: ```text
84: /memory:save 042-my-feature
85: ```
86: 
87: The `/memory:save` command's generate-context lane refreshes `description.json.lastUpdated` and `graph-metadata.json.derived.*`. Direct MCP `memory_save({ filePath })` indexes content only; on mutating packet-doc saves it returns a `metadataRefresh` advisory with `refreshed:false` and points callers back to the generate-context save lane when packet metadata must be current.
88: 
89: ### Resume Work From a Previous Session
90: 
91: Start a new session on work you did before:
92: 
93: ```text
94: /speckit:resume
95: ```
96: 
97: The system first resolves the requested folder. If it is a phase parent with a valid `derived.last_active_child_id`, resume follows that pointer into the active child before comparing folder-local `handover.md` and `_memory.continuity` in `implementation-summary.md`; it then falls back to the packet's canonical spec docs. It presents the current state, prior decisions, touched files and next steps before you start.
98: 
99: ### Search for Context
100: 
101: Ask the indexed-continuity store a question in plain language:
102: 
103: ```text
104: /memory:search "how did we decide on the auth architecture?"
105: ```
106: 
107: The system reads your question, figures out you are looking for a past decision and routes to the right search strategy automatically.
108: 
109: ### Validate a Spec Folder
110: 
111: ```bash
112: # Run all 20 validation rules
113: bash .opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/scripts/spec/validate.sh \
114:   .opencode/specs/[project]/042-my-feature/
115: 
116: # Exit 0 = success, Exit 1 = user error, Exit 2 = validation error, Exit 3 = system error
117: ```
118: 
119: ### Verify the MCP Is Running
120: 
121: Check that Spec Kit Memory tools are available:
122: 
123: ```json
124: {
125:   "tool": "memory_health",
126:   "arguments": { "reportMode": "full" }
127: }
128: ```
129: 
130: The response should return `status: "ok"` and database table counts. If it returns an error, see [Troubleshooting](#7-troubleshooting).
131: 
132: The same check works from a shell through the daemon-backed CLI, which fronts the identical 39-tool surface:
133: 
134: ```bash
135: # Enumerate the tools without touching the daemon
136: node .opencode/bin/spec-memory.cjs list-tools --format text
137: 
138: # Call a tool against the live daemon (auto-spawns it when cold)
139: node .opencode/bin/spec-memory.cjs memory_health --json '{"reportMode":"full"}' --format json
140: ```
141: 
142: Exit codes: `0` success, `1` runtime error, `64` usage/schema error, `69` protocol/dist mismatch, `75` retryable daemon error. Pass `--warm-only` in prompt-time contexts so a cold daemon yields exit `75` instead of a cold spawn.
143: 
144: Codex CLI note: if the MCP server runs in a restricted or read-only repo context, point `SPEC_KIT_DB_DIR` at a writable directory such as one under your home folder or `/tmp`. Use `MEMORY_DB_PATH` only when you intentionally need one fixed sqlite file.
145: 
146: ---
147: 
148: ## 4. FEATURES
149: 
150: ### 4.1 SPEC FOLDER WORKFLOWS
151: 
152: #### What Spec Folders Are
153: 
154: A spec folder is a numbered directory (like `specs/042-my-feature/`) that holds the documentation for a single unit of work. Think of it as a project folder for an AI conversation. It keeps the specification, plan, task list and implementation summary together so the reasoning behind every change is preserved. For packet roots and direct child phases, closeout can also produce packet-local changelog entries that live beside the packet instead of only in the global release stream.
155: 
156: Every conversation that modifies files gets a spec folder. This is enforced by Gate 3 in the project's AGENTS.md. The AI assistant asks "Which spec folder?" before any file modification begins. The only exemptions are single-file fixes under 5 characters (typo or whitespace corrections).
157: 
158: #### Documentation Levels
159: 
160: Not every change needs the same amount of paperwork. A one-line bug fix does not need an architecture decision record. A multi-system refactor does. Spec Kit uses four levels to match documentation depth to task complexity.
161: 
162: | Level  | LOC Guidance   | Required Files                                        | When to Use                                          |
163: | ------ | -------------- | ----------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------- |
164: | **1**  | < 100          | spec.md, plan.md, tasks.md, implementation-summary.md | Small features, bug fixes, single-file changes       |
165: | **2**  | 100 - 499      | Level 1 + checklist.md                                | Features needing QA verification, multi-file changes |
166: | **3**  | 500+           | Level 2 + decision-record.md                          | Architecture changes, complex refactors              |
167: | **3+** | Complexity 80+ | Level 3 + approval workflow, compliance, stakeholders  | High-complexity work needing review tracking          |
168: | **Phase Parent** | n/a (control file only) | spec.md, description.json, graph-metadata.json | Folder contains phase children (`[0-9]{3}-name/` subdirs with their own spec.md/description.json) |
169: 
170: The LOC ranges are guidance, not hard rules. Risk, complexity and the number of affected files can push a task to a higher level. When in doubt, choose the higher level.
171: 
172: **Implementation-summary.md** is required at all levels but created **after** implementation completes, not at spec folder creation time.
173: 
174: Packet-local changelogs are additive, not a replacement for `implementation-summary.md`. When the target is a packet root or direct child phase, `/speckit:implement`, `/speckit:complete` and the nested changelog workflow can write packet history into a local `changelog/` directory using the canonical root/phase naming rules.
175: 
176: #### Spec Folder Structure
177: 
178: When `create.sh` builds a spec folder, it produces this layout:
179: 
180: ```text
181: specs/<###-feature-name>/
182: ├── description.json             # Spec identity and memory tracking metadata
183: ├── spec.md                      # What the feature is and why it exists
184: ├── plan.md                      # How to implement it
185: ├── tasks.md                     # Step-by-step task breakdown
186: ├── checklist.md                 # QA validation gates (Level 2+)
187: ├── decision-record.md           # Architecture decisions (Level 3+)
188: ├── implementation-summary.md    # Post-implementation summary (all levels)
189: ├── handover.md                  # Operator-facing session handoff for /speckit:resume
190: ├── resource-map.md              # Optional lean path catalog (any level)
191: ├── changelog/                   # Packet-local changelog history for packet roots / phase parents
192: └── scratch/                     # Temporary workspace files (gitignored)
193: ```
194: 
195: `generate-context.js` updates the packet's continuity state for `/speckit:resume`, refreshes `description.json.lastUpdated` and rewrites `graph-metadata.json` derived fields on every generate-context save-lane run. Direct MCP `memory_save({ filePath })` does not refresh those metadata files; its success response includes a `metadataRefresh` advisory when packet metadata may now lag. Recovery then compares folder-local `handover.md` and `_memory.continuity` freshness, with packet docs as fallback.
196: 
197: **Phase parents** are an exception. When a folder contains phase children (matching `^[0-9]{3}-[a-z0-9-]+$` with their own `spec.md` or `description.json`), the parent only requires the **lean trio**: `spec.md`, `description.json`, `graph-metadata.json`. Heavy docs (`plan.md`, `tasks.md`, `checklist.md`, `decision-record.md`, `implementation-summary.md`) live exclusively in the children where they stay accurate to that phase's actual work. The parent's `spec.md` carries a Phase Documentation Map. The parent's `graph-metadata.json` carries `derived.last_active_child_id` + `derived.last_active_at` pointer fields that the generator atomically updates on every save (parent saves write `null`, child saves bubble up the child's `packet_id`). `/speckit:resume` reads `derived.last_active_child_id` first when the target is a phase parent and follows valid bare child ids (`001-phase`) or track-relative child paths under the parent. The redirect is bounded and escape-safe; missing, malformed, stale-to-missing-child, or non-child pointers leave resume on the requested folder instead of escaping the packet tree. Detection is a single source of truth: `is_phase_parent()` (shell) and `isPhaseParent()` (ESM JS) MUST agree.
198: 
199: #### Checklist Priority System (Level 2+)
200: 
201: Checklists use a priority system so reviewers know what blocks shipping and what can wait:
202: 
203: | Priority | Meaning                                                 | Deferral                        |
204: | -------- | ------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------- |
205: | **P0**   | Hard blocker, cannot ship without this                | Cannot defer                    |
206: | **P1**   | Required, must complete or get user approval to defer | Needs explicit approval to skip |
207: | **P2**   | Optional, nice to have                                | Can defer without approval      |
208: 
209: #### Phase Decomposition
210: 
211: When a feature is too large for a single spec folder, use phase decomposition to split it into parent and child folders. The parent holds the overall specification. Each child holds one phase of the work.
212: 
213: ```text
214: specs/022-big-feature/             # Parent spec folder
215: ├── spec.md                        # Overall specification
216: ├── 001-data-model/                # Phase 1 child
217: │   ├── spec.md
218: │   └── ...
219: ├── 002-api-endpoints/             # Phase 2 child
220: │   ├── spec.md
221: │   └── ...
222: └── 003-frontend/                  # Phase 3 child
223:     ├── spec.md
224:     └── ...
225: ```
226: 
227: Use `create.sh --phase` to create a parent with its first child in one step. The parent and child folders render from the manifest template source through the Level contract resolver. Run `validate.sh --recursive` to validate the parent and all children together. The validator's phase-parent branch automatically skips Level-N expectations on the lean parent (rules with branches: `check-files.sh`, `check-level-match.sh`, `check-anchors.sh`, `check-section-counts.sh`, `check-template-headers.sh`).
228: 
229: Tolerant migration policy: legacy phase parents that retain heavy docs continue to validate without churn. Soft deprecation is a separate follow-on packet.
230: 
231: #### Validation
232: 
233: The `validate.sh` script runs 20+ rules against a spec folder and reports what passes and what needs fixing. Rules check for required files, template compliance, placeholder detection, anchor markers and cross-reference consistency. The new `PHASE_PARENT_CONTENT` rule (severity: warn) scans phase-parent `spec.md` for forbidden migration-history tokens (consolidation/merge/rename narratives) and is code-fence + HTML-comment aware. In strict flows, the validation surface includes `_memory.continuity` freshness checks plus strict `EVIDENCE` marker linting, with the bracket-depth audit script available for repair sweeps before rerunning validation.
234: 
235: | Exit Code | Meaning          | Action                              |
236: | --------- | ---------------- | ----------------------------------- |
237: | 0         | All rules pass   | Ready to proceed                    |
238: | 1         | User error       | Fix the flag or input and retry     |
239: | 2         | Validation error | Fix the spec folder before claiming completion |
240: | 3         | System error     | Check file I/O, missing manifest or environment |
241: 
242: Run with `--verbose` to see the details behind each rule or `--recursive` to validate a parent and all child phase folders.
243:

245: 
246: <!-- divider:4.2 -->
247: 
248: ### 4.2 MEMORY SYSTEM
249: 
250: The indexed-continuity store lives in an MCP server that gives AI assistants persistent memory across sessions, models and tools. It stores context in a local SQLite database and retrieves exactly what is relevant when a new session starts.
251: 
252: Think of it like a personal librarian that keeps notes on every conversation, files them by topic and hands you the right ones when you start a new task. Switch from Claude to GPT and back. The spec-doc record stays the same because it lives on your machine, not inside any AI's context window.
253: 
254: For full architecture details, the 39-tool API reference, search pipeline internals and configuration, see [`mcp_server/README.md`](./mcp_server/README.md).
255: 
256: #### Dual-Stack Access: MCP and CLI
257: 
258: The memory surface is dual-stack. The `mk-spec-memory` MCP registration stays the native in-session path today, and `node .opencode/bin/spec-memory.cjs` is a full-parity CLI front door over the **same daemon** with the identical 39 tools — nothing about the daemon changed, only the IPC transport. Use MCP for live in-session calls. Use the CLI for hooks, cron jobs, CI, operator shell diagnostics and transport-down recovery: when an MCP transport drops mid-session and the client never reconnects it, the CLI still reaches every tool. Prompt-time callers must probe warm-only first; exit `75` means retryable daemon or IPC unavailability. `list-tools` answers offline; every other command speaks JSON-RPC to the daemon over the IPC socket. Shared exit taxonomy across the three sibling CLIs (`spec-memory`, `code-index`, `skill-advisor`): `0`/`1`/`64`/`69`/`75`. The shim refuses to run a stale build (exit `69`; `SPECKIT_SPEC_MEMORY_CLI_DEV_ALLOW_STALE=1` for dev loops), and `--warm-only` plus the prompt-time env flags keep prompt-time hooks from ever cold-spawning the daemon. Because this CLI already has full parity, a later evolution could make the CLI the primary or sole transport without breaking existing MCP workflows; that is a possible direction, not a committed plan. See [`mcp_server/ENV_REFERENCE.md`](./mcp_server/ENV_REFERENCE.md) for the CLI env-flag table.
259: 
260: #### Hybrid Search
261: 
262: When you search, the system checks five sources at once, like a librarian who checks the card catalog, the shelf labels, the reading room sign-out sheet, the recommendation board and the "related topics" corkboard all at the same time.
263: 
264: | Channel          | How It Works                                        | Good For                                       |
265: | ---------------- | --------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------- |
266: | **Vector**       | Compares meaning via embeddings (`nomic-embed-text-v1.5` 768d local default) | Finding related content even when words differ |
267: | **FTS5**         | Full-text search on exact words and phrases         | Specific terms and error messages              |
268: | **BM25**         | Keyword relevance scoring                           | Ranking when you know roughly what you want    |
269: | **Causal Graph** | Follows cause-and-effect links between memories     | "Why did we choose this?" questions            |
270: | **Degree**       | Scores by graph connectivity, weighted by edge type | Finding important hub decisions                |
271: 
272: Results from all channels are combined using Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) with a K parameter tuned per query intent. A spec-doc record that scores well in multiple channels rises to the top.
273: 
274: #### Search Pipeline
275: 
276: Every search goes through four stages, like an assembly line where each station has one clear job:
277: 
278: 1. **Gather**: collect candidates from active channels in parallel. Constitutional memories are always injected regardless of score.
279: 2. **Score**: fuse channel results with RRF, then apply the post-fusion scoring signals in one authoritative pass (session boost, recency fusion, causal boost, co-activation spreading, community co-retrieval, graph signals, FSRS testing effect, intent weights, artifact routing, feedback signals, anchor and validation metadata enrichment). Intent weights are applied here only for non-hybrid search, so hybrid results are never double-weighted.
280: 3. **Rerank**: apply MMR diversity reranking (algorithmic, no model) to reduce near-duplicate results, then collapse chunks back to parent memories.
281: 4. **Filter**: enforce score immutability, apply state filtering, annotate with confidence labels (high/medium/low) and truncate at the confidence gap.
282: 
283: #### Query Intelligence
284: 
285: Before any search runs, the system figures out what kind of help you need, like a triage nurse who reads your symptoms and routes you to the right specialist.
286: 
287: - **Complexity routing** sizes up your question and picks how many channels to use (2 for simple, 4 for moderate, all 5 for complex)
288: - **Intent classification** maps your query to one of 7 task types (add_feature, fix_bug, refactor, security_audit, understand, find_spec, find_decision), each with its own channel weight profile
289: - **Query decomposition** splits multi-topic questions into focused sub-queries without needing an LLM call
290: - **HyDE fallback** writes a hypothetical answer to your question, then searches for real documents matching it, surfacing content your original wording missed
291: 
292: #### Memory Lifecycle
293: 
294: Not all spec-doc records are equally useful forever. The system uses FSRS (Free Spaced Repetition Scheduler) to track freshness, a decay model validated on 100M+ Anki flashcard users.
295: 
296: | Tier               | Description                       | Decay Behavior                           |
297: | ------------------ | --------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
298: | **Constitutional** | Always-surface rules (3.0x boost) | Never decays                             |
299: | **Critical**       | High-importance decisions         | Never decays or decays at 2x slower rate |
300: | **Important**      | Significant patterns              | 1.5x slower than normal                  |
301: | **Normal**         | Standard session context          | Standard FSRS decay                      |
302: | **Temporary**      | Quick scratch notes               | Fast decay                               |
303: | **Deprecated**     | Superseded content                | Fastest decay                            |
304: 
305: Decay speed is also controlled by content type (decisions decay slower than general notes). Memories earn promotions through positive feedback: 5 thumbs-up promotes normal to important, 10 promotes to critical.
306: 
307: Four active cognitive states track access patterns: **HOT** (just used), **WARM**, **COLD** and **DORMANT**. Hot memories get full content in results. Warm ones appear as summaries. Cold and dormant content only surfaces if it still scores well enough.
308: 
309: #### Causal Graph
310: 
311: The system tracks how decisions relate to each other, like a corkboard with sticky notes connected by string. One note says "we chose JWT tokens." A string connects it to "because the session store was too slow." Another string connects that to "the Redis outage on March 5th."
312: 
313: Six relationship types: `caused`, `enabled`, `supersedes`, `contradicts`, `derived_from`, `supports`. Community detection (Louvain algorithm) automatically clusters related memories so finding one surfaces its neighbors.
314: 
315: #### Causal Trust Display Badges (012/005)
316: 
317: `memory_search` results now carry an additive `trustBadges` payload per `MemoryResultEnvelope`. The badges read existing causal-edge columns at response time, so callers can judge whether a causal claim looks fresh and well-supported without changing storage.
318: 
319: | Badge | Source |
320: |-------|--------|
321: | `confidence` | clamped from the strongest connected edge `strength` |
322: | `extractionAge` | human-readable age from the newest connected `extracted_at` |
323: | `lastAccessAge` | human-readable age from the newest connected `last_accessed` |
324: | `orphan` | `true` when the result has no incoming causal edges |
325: | `weightHistoryChanged` | `true` when any connected edge has a `weight_history` row |
326: 
327: The formatter at `mcp_server/formatters/search-results.ts` batch-derives the badges from existing causal-edge tables, fails open when the DB handle or `weight_history` table is unavailable and preserves any precomputed `trustBadges` payload a caller already supplied. Response-profile shaping in `mcp_server/lib/response/profile-formatters.ts` preserves the badge payload through `quick`, `research` and `resume` outputs on `results[]` and `topResult` rather than dropping it. Display only: no schema change, no new relation types, no new storage of code/process/tool facts (ADR-012-005).
328: 
329: #### Save Intelligence
330: 
331: When you save new content, the system runs an arbitration process before storing anything. Prediction Error gating compares incoming content against existing spec-doc records and picks one of four outcomes:
332: 
333: | Outcome       | When                               | What Happens                                |
334: | ------------- | ---------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------- |
335: | **CREATE**    | Nothing similar exists             | Stored as new knowledge                     |
336: | **REINFORCE** | Similar exists, new one adds value | Both kept, existing gets a confidence boost |
337: | **UPDATE**    | Similar exists, new one is better  | Old version replaced in place               |
338: | **SUPERSEDE** | New knowledge contradicts the old  | New version active, old one demoted         |
339: 
340: Three quality gates run before storage: structure check (required format and metadata), semantic sufficiency check (enough real content to be useful) and duplicate detection.
341: Short decision-type memories can bypass the content-length gate when SPECKIT_SAVE_QUALITY_GATE_EXCEPTIONS=true and at least two structural signals (title, specFolder or anchor) are present.
342: 
343: #### Embedding Reconciliation
344: 
345: `memory_embedding_reconcile` is a maintenance tool on the mk-spec-memory surface. It converges `embedding_status` for vector-present stale rows and resets genuinely missing-vector retry rows inside one guarded `BEGIN IMMEDIATE` transaction. The default mode is `dry-run`: no writes happen unless `mode: "apply"` is passed. Use it after an embedder swap or after the health report shows a high pending/failed count.
346: 
347: ```json
348: { "tool": "memory_embedding_reconcile", "arguments": { "mode": "dry-run" } }
349: ```
350: 
351: #### Index Scan Self-Maintaining Behavior
352: 
353: `memory_index_scan` is self-maintaining from spec 026 onward. Overlapping scan calls return a `coalesced: true` success envelope instead of a raw E429 error. Rows become BM25/FTS-searchable immediately as `pending` while vectors drain (`complete_with_pending_vectors` with a `pendingVectors` count), so content is always text-searchable even when the embedding queue is backed up. Move reconciliation heals renamed spec folders by packet identity without re-embedding. Each scan also runs a bounded global orphan sweep.
354: 
355: `memory_health` now includes an `index` block with a `summary` enum and row counts:
356: 
357: | Summary value | Meaning |
358: | --- | --- |
359: | `healthy_fresh` | Index is current and all vectors are resolved |
360: | `healthy_lagging_vectors` | Index is current but some vectors are still pending |
361: | `stale_needs_scan` | Index has not been scanned recently |
362: | `degraded_needs_repair` | Failed rows require `memory_embedding_reconcile` |
363: | `unavailable` | Index state could not be read |
364: 
365: #### Index Schema History (v34 -> v37) and the `.needs-rebuild` Sentinel
366: 
367: The SQLite index schema (`mcp_server/lib/search/vector-index-schema.ts`, `SCHEMA_VERSION = 37`) advanced four migrations during the shipped memory hardening work. Each is additive and applied automatically at server boot:
368: 
369: | Migration | Adds | Effect |
370: | --- | --- | --- |
371: | **v34** | `memory_trigger_embeddings` table and status index | Stores derived trigger-phrase embeddings for default-off semantic trigger shadow matching. Lexical trigger matching remains primary unless the semantic trigger flags are explicitly enabled. |
372: | **v35** | `memory_index.source_kind` with provenance backfill | Normalizes saved row provenance into `human`, `agent`, `system`, `import` or `feedback` while preserving safe defaults for older rows. |
373: | **v36** | `memory_idempotency_receipts`, `delete_after`, `near_duplicate_of`, `last_dedup_checked_at` | Adds server-derived replay receipts for save/update paths and advisory near-duplicate hints without making the feature active unless `SPECKIT_MEMORY_IDEMPOTENCY=true`. |
374: | **v37** | `deleted_at`, active recall index, purgeable retention index | Adds tombstone-ready partitions so delete and retention paths can use soft-delete tombstones when `SPECKIT_SOFT_DELETE_TOMBSTONES=true`. |
375: 
376: After a checkpoint restore that swaps the live DB files, the runtime writes a `.needs-rebuild` sentinel (`NEEDS_REBUILD_SENTINEL_NAME` in `mcp_server/lib/storage/checkpoints.ts`) beside the restored DB. The next boot detects it through `repairNeedsRebuildSentinel()` and rebuilds the derived indexes (FTS5/BM25 shadow and vector profile) before serving, so a restored snapshot never serves from a stale shadow. The sentinel is cleared once the rebuild completes.
377: 
378: #### Memory Hardening and Observability
379: 
380: The shipped memory-hardening surface is intentionally conservative. Semantic trigger scoring, feedback retention learning, session-trace causal inference, idempotency receipts, soft-delete tombstones and completion freshness all default OFF. When enabled, the first step is shadow, audit or advisory output so operators can compare behavior before changing live recall or retention.
381: 
382: | Feature | Default | Operator-facing behavior |
383: | --- | --- | --- |
384: | Semantic-trigger shadow | OFF | Computes semantic trigger candidates while lexical triggers remain primary; `union` mode still requires the master flag. |
385: | Idempotency and provenance | OFF for receipts; `source_kind` always present | Adds replay receipts and near-duplicate hints only when enabled; provenance is normalized as `human`, `agent`, `system`, `import` or `feedback`. |
386: | Soft-delete tombstones | OFF | Adds tombstone-aware delete and retention partitions behind `SPECKIT_SOFT_DELETE_TOMBSTONES`. Keep OFF until recall filters tombstoned rows consistently. |
387: | Retrieval observability | OFF by default | `SPECKIT_RESPONSE_TRACE=true` adds search trace payloads for debugging without changing the default concise response shape. |
388: | Feedback reducers | OFF | Session-trace causal inference and feedback-aware retention run only behind explicit gates; retention is shadow-first unless active mode has evidence. |
389: | Completion freshness | OFF | Strict validation can compare stored continuity fingerprints with packet content and optionally promote stale findings to errors. |
390: 
391: Stale-audit and tool-ownership lint are live guardrails around this surface: health checks report stale conditions, and pre-commit compares the generated tool-ownership map against live `TOOL_DEFINITIONS` so command ownership cannot drift silently.
392: 
393: #### MCP Front-Proxy and In-Place Daemon Recycle
394: 
395: The mk-spec-memory launcher fronts the backend daemon with a session proxy (`bridgeStdioThroughSessionProxy` in `.opencode/bin/mk-spec-memory-launcher.cjs`, implemented in `.opencode/bin/lib/launcher-session-proxy.cjs`). The proxy keeps one stable client-facing stdio session while the backend behind it can be recycled in place, for example when the RSS-ceiling watchdog (`SPECKIT_LAUNCHER_RSS_SELF_EXIT`) restarts the daemon or when a new build replaces the backend. Read-only replayable tools (`memory_search`, `memory_context` and similar) are transparently retried across a recycle so a routine restart looks like a brief pause rather than a hard failure to the caller.
396: 
397: This recycle/reconnect behavior surfaces three operator-visible error codes:
398: 
399: | Code | Retryable | Meaning |
400: | --- | --- | --- |
401: | `E429` | Legacy | The former index rate-limit class. The self-maintaining `memory_index_scan` contract replaced it with a `coalesced: true` success envelope, so a routine overlapping scan no longer returns `E429`. |
402: | `-32001` | **Yes** | `RETRYABLE_RECYCLE_ERROR`, the **live** launcher recycle signal (`backend recycled; retry`, `data.retryable: true`). The front-proxy emits it when the backend is recycling under it, and clients retry and reconnect. This code is **not removed**: only the index vector-drain *outage* path stopped surfacing its own `-32001` class. |
403: | `-32002` | No | `PROTOCOL_MISMATCH_ERROR`, a fail-closed protocol break (`backend protocol version changed; client reconnect required`, `data.retryable: false`). The proxy moves to a terminal CLOSED state and the client must reconnect from scratch, and it is never auto-retried. |
404: 
405: #### Evaluation Infrastructure
406: 
407: The indexed-continuity store includes built-in tools for measuring search quality:
408: 
409: - **Ablation studies**: turn off one search component at a time to measure its contribution, like removing one ingredient from a recipe to see if the dish still tastes good
410: - **12-metric computation**: MRR, NDCG, MAP and 9 other information retrieval metrics
411: - **Synthetic ground truth corpus**: 110 test questions with known correct answers for benchmarking, keyed to live parent-memory IDs. Rerun `scripts/evals/map-ground-truth-ids.ts` after DB rebuilds or imports before trusting ablation or reporting comparisons
412: - **Reporting dashboard**: performance trends across work periods and search channels, sourced from parent-memory-normalized eval rows even when retrieval hits came from chunks
413:

418: ### 4.3 COMMANDS
419: 
420: Spec Kit exposes its core workflow through the `/speckit:*` commands (`complete`, `implement`, `plan` and `resume`, where `plan` also has an `--intake-only` variant) plus the `/memory:*` operations. Repository-wide command entry points also include the `/deep:*` loop commands (one of which, `agent-improvement`, supersedes the former `improve` commands), the `/create:*` commands, the `/doctor` diagnostics and the `agent_router` and `prompt` utilities. Each command opens access to a specific set of tools.
421: 
422: #### Spec Kit Commands
423: 
424: | Command                 | Steps | Purpose                                                                                                                          |
425: | ----------------------- | ----- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
426: | `/speckit:plan --intake-only` | N/A   | Standalone intake interview that publishes `spec.md`, `description.json` and `graph-metadata.json`                        |
427: | `/speckit:complete`    | 14    | Full end-to-end workflow: spec through implementation, verification and packet-local changelog closeout, with the shared intake contract from [intake_contract.md](./references/workflows/intake_contract.md) when intake is still needed |
428: | `/speckit:plan`        | 7     | Planning only: spec through plan, no implementation, with the shared intake contract from [intake_contract.md](./references/workflows/intake_contract.md) for `no-spec`, `partial-folder`, `repair-mode` or `placeholder-upgrade` packets |
429: | `/speckit:implement`   | 9     | Execute pre-planned work. Requires existing `plan.md`. Packet-aware targets also generate local changelog output during closeout |
430: | `/speckit:resume`      | 4     | Resume a previous session on an existing spec folder                                                                             |
431: | `/deep:research` | N/A | Autonomous research loop with convergence detection plus bounded `spec.md` anchoring under [spec_check_protocol.md](../deep-loop-workflows/deep-research/references/protocol/spec_check_protocol.md) |
432: | `/deep:review` | N/A   | Autonomous code review loop with convergence detection                                                                           |
433: 
434: **Mode Suffixes** change how commands run:
435: 
436: | Suffix           | Behavior                                                                                              |
437: | ---------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
438: | `:auto`          | Execute without approval gates                                                                        |
439: | `:confirm`       | Pause at each step for approval                                                                       |
440: | `:with-phases`   | Phase decomposition mode on planning / completion flows, not a standalone command                     |
441: | `:with-research` | Dispatch deep research before verification (`/speckit:complete` only)                               |
442: 
443: **Command source files**: `.opencode/commands/speckit/`
444: 
445: #### Memory Commands
446: 
447: | Command          | Tool Count | Purpose                                                                                                                                 |
448: | ---------------- | ---------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
449: | `/memory:save`   | 4          | Update packet continuity surfaces and supporting generated context artifacts with semantic indexing                                      |
450: | `/memory:search` | 13         | Search, retrieve and analyze knowledge. Auto-detects task intent from 7 types                                                           |
451: | `/memory:manage` | 20         | Database maintenance and lifecycle operations: stats, scan, cleanup, bulk-delete, checkpoints and ingest |
452: | `/memory:learn`  | 6          | Constitutional memory manager: create, list, edit, remove always-surface rules                                                          |
453: 
454: Session recovery lives in `/speckit:resume`, which compares folder-local `handover.md` and `_memory.continuity` in `implementation-summary.md`, selects the fresher source, then falls back to canonical spec docs before deeper memory retrieval is needed.
455: 
456: Some commands own their tools (they are the primary home) while others borrow tools from `/memory:search` or `/memory:manage`. A borrowed tool works the same way, it is just administered somewhere else.
457: 
458: **Command source files**: `.opencode/commands/memory/`
459:

463: 
464: ### 4.4 TEMPLATES
465: 
466: #### Manifest Architecture
467: 
468: Templates live in one manifest source and render through the Level contract resolver. `create.sh` asks the resolver which files belong to Level 1, Level 2, Level 3, Level 3+ or phase-parent packets, then the inline renderer expands only the sections allowed for that Level.
469: 
470: ```text
471: Level 1:  spec.md, plan.md, tasks.md, implementation-summary.md
472: Level 2:  Level 1 + checklist.md
473: Level 3:  Level 2 + decision-record.md
474: Level 3+: Level 3 + extended governance sections
475: Phase:    lean parent trio plus child phase folders
476: ```
477: 
478: Optional support documents such as `handover.md`, `debug-delegation.md`, `research.md` and `resource-map.md` are rendered by the workflow that owns them. New spec folders should be created through the CLI:
479: 
480: ```bash
481: bash .opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/scripts/spec/create.sh --level 2 --path /tmp/example-spec --name "example spec"
482: ```
483: 
484: Set `SPECKIT_POST_VALIDATE=1` when a strict workflow should run full validation immediately after scaffolding. The default path skips full post-create validation so normal scaffolds stay fast.
485: 
486: `create.sh` rejects `--path` values that traverse outside the repository (for example `--path "../etc/foo"`) with a clear error before any filesystem write happens.
487: 
488: A `mkdir`-based advisory lock protects `description.json` and `graph-metadata.json` writes during canonical save, so two parallel `/memory:save` calls for the same packet do not race.
489: 
490: #### Template Compliance
491: 
492: Templates use ANCHOR markers (`<!-- ANCHOR:section --> ... <!-- /ANCHOR:section -->`) to mark logical sections. Validation checks for required anchors, proper section ordering and template version alignment. The `template_compliance_contract.md` reference defines which anchors are required at each level.
493: 
494: ---
495: 
496: <!-- divider:4.5 -->
497: 
498: ### 4.5 SCRIPTS AND VALIDATION
499: 
500: #### Spec Management Scripts
501: 
502: The `scripts/spec/` directory holds the scripts that manage the full lifecycle of spec folders:
503: 
504: | Script                        | Purpose                                                                                        |
505: | ----------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
506: | `create.sh`                   | Create spec folders with level-appropriate templates. Use `--phase` for parent + child folders |
507: | `validate.sh`                 | Run 20 validation rules. Use `--recursive` for phase folders, `--verbose` for details          |
508: | `upgrade-level.sh`            | Render additional Level contract sections for a higher documentation level                     |
509: | `recommend-level.sh`          | Analyze scope and risk to recommend the right documentation level                              |
510: | `calculate-completeness.sh`   | Calculate spec folder completeness as a percentage                                             |
511: | `check-completion.sh`         | Verify all completion criteria are met                                                         |
512: | `check-placeholders.sh`       | Find remaining `[PLACEHOLDER]` values after level upgrade                                      |
513: | `check-template-staleness.sh` | Detect templates that need regeneration                                                        |
514: | `progressive-validate.sh`     | Progressive validation for in-progress work                                                    |
515: | `quality-audit.sh`            | Run quality audit on spec folder content                                                       |
516: | `archive.sh`                  | Archive completed spec folders                                                                 |
517: | `test-validation.sh`          | Test the validation rules themselves                                                           |
518: 
519: #### Memory Scripts
520: 
521: The `scripts/memory/` directory holds the scripts for the indexed-continuity store:
522: 
523: | Script                        | Purpose                                                     |
524: | ----------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------- |
525: | `generate-context.ts`         | Source for the runtime memory-save entrypoint `scripts/dist/memory/generate-context.js` |
526: | `backfill-frontmatter.ts`     | Add missing frontmatter to existing generated context artifacts |
527: | `backfill-research-metadata.ts` | Backfill missing `description.json` and `graph-metadata.json` files under `research/*/iterations/` |
528: | `rank-memories.ts`            | Rank memories by relevance for a query                      |
529: | `reindex-embeddings.ts`       | Rebuild embedding vectors for stored spec-doc records               |
530: | `cleanup-orphaned-vectors.ts` | Remove vector entries with no matching spec-doc record               |
531: | `rebuild-auto-entities.ts`    | Regenerate auto-extracted entity catalog                    |
532: | `validate-memory-quality.ts`  | Run quality checks on stored spec-doc record content                 |
533: | `ast-parser.ts`               | Parse markdown AST for section extraction                   |
534: | `fix-memory-h1.mjs`           | Fix heading levels in older generated context artifacts     |
535: 
536: TypeScript sources compile to `scripts/dist/`. The runtime entry point for memory saves is `scripts/dist/memory/generate-context.js`.
537: 
538: #### Validation Helper Scripts
539: 
540: The `scripts/validation/` directory contains focused helpers that support `validate.sh` and one-off remediation work:
541: 
542: | Script                        | Purpose                                                                 |
543: | ----------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------- |
544: | `continuity-freshness.ts`     | Warn when `_memory.continuity.last_updated_at` lags `graph-metadata.json` |
545: | `evidence-marker-audit.ts`    | Bracket-depth audit and optional rewrap pass for malformed `EVIDENCE` markers |
546: | `evidence-marker-lint.ts`     | Strict wrapper used by validation to fail on malformed or unclosed markers |
547: 
548: #### Template Rendering
549: 
550: Template changes flow through the manifest source, Level contract resolver and inline renderer. Use `create.sh` for generated packet fixtures and `validate.sh` for explicit validation.
551: 
552: ---
553: 
554: ## 5. STRUCTURE
555: 
556: ```
557: .opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/
558: ├── SKILL.md                    # AI workflow instructions (when to use, gates, rules)
559: ├── README.md                   # This file (what it does, how to use it)
560: ├── ARCHITECTURE.md             # Boundary contract: scripts/ vs mcp_server/
561: ├── templates/                  # Manifest template source
562: │   └── manifest/               # Rendered by Level contract resolver + inline renderer
563: ├── scripts/                    # CLI tools (TypeScript source + Bash)
564: │   ├── spec/                   # Spec folder management scripts
565: │   ├── memory/                 # Memory system scripts
566: │   ├── templates/              # Template composition (manifest renderer)
567: │   ├── core/                   # Core library (17 modules)
568: │   ├── extractors/             # Session data extractors (12 extractors)
569: │   ├── utils/                  # Utility modules (20 utilities)
570: │   └── dist/                   # Compiled JavaScript output
571: ├── mcp_server/                 # Spec Kit Memory MCP (TypeScript)
572: │   ├── context-server.ts       # MCP server entry point and tool registration
573: │   ├── handlers/               # Tool handlers, save pipeline, and response assembly
574: │   ├── lib/                    # Search pipeline, cognitive engine, graph, governance
575: │   ├── matrix_runners/         # F1-F14 x CLI adapter manifest and runner
576: │   ├── stress_test/            # Opt-in stress, load, matrix-cell, and degraded-state suites
577: │   ├── tests/                  # MCP test suite
578: │   ├── INSTALL_GUIDE.md        # Full installation walkthrough
579: │   └── README.md               # MCP server reference (tool API, pipeline, configuration)
580: ├── shared/                     # Shared workspace (@spec-kit/shared)
581: │   ├── algorithms/             # Fusion, reranking, and lab algorithms
582: │   ├── contracts/              # Typed trace/envelope contracts
583: │   ├── embeddings/             # Provider implementations
584: │   └── ...                     # Chunker, scoring, parsing, utilities
585: ├── references/                 # Reference documentation (27 files)
586: ├── assets/                     # Decision matrices, YAML configs
587: ├── constitutional/             # Always-surface rules (never decay)
588: ├── feature_catalog/            # Feature documentation catalog
589: └── manual_testing_playbook/    # Manual validation scenarios
590: ```
591: 
592: ### Key Files
593: 
594: | File                                                                         | Purpose                                                                                              |
595: | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
596: | [`SKILL.md`](./SKILL.md)                                                     | AI agent instructions: routing rules, gates, validation procedures, template application             |
597: | [`README.md`](./README.md)                                                   | This file: what Spec Kit does, how to use it, where to find things                                 |
598: | [`ARCHITECTURE.md`](./ARCHITECTURE.md)                                       | API boundary contract between `scripts/` and `mcp_server/`                                           |
599: | [`mcp_server/README.md`](./mcp_server/README.md)                             | Full MCP architecture: 39-tool API reference, search pipeline, graph intelligence and configuration |
600: | [`mcp_server/INSTALL_GUIDE.md`](./mcp_server/INSTALL_GUIDE.md)               | Step-by-step installation with embedding providers and environment                                   |
601: | [`scripts/spec/create.sh`](./scripts/spec/create.sh)                         | Create spec folders with level-appropriate template files                                            |
602: | [`scripts/spec/validate.sh`](./scripts/spec/validate.sh)                     | Run 20-rule validation on any spec folder                                                            |
603: | `scripts/dist/memory/generate-context.js`                                    | Primary workflow for updating packet continuity state from structured JSON                            |
604: | [`feature_catalog/feature_catalog.md`](./feature_catalog/feature_catalog.md) | Complete catalog of implemented features                                    |
605: 
606: ### How the Pieces Connect
607: 
608: Think of Spec Kit as a filing system with a librarian attached.
609: 
610: The **spec folder workflow** is the filing system. Every time you modify files, it creates a numbered folder with the right paperwork (specification, plan, tasks). Templates make sure every folder follows the same structure. Validation checks that nothing is missing.
611: 
612: The **memory system** is the librarian. When a session ends, `generate-context.js` updates the packet's canonical continuity surfaces so the next session can recover from packet-local sources first. The MCP server indexes those packet docs into vector, FTS5 and BM25 surfaces, while graph and degree signals are computed at retrieval time. When a new session starts, `/speckit:resume` compares folder-local `handover.md` and `_memory.continuity` in `implementation-summary.md`, selects the fresher source, then falls back to the packet docs. If you need deeper retrieval after that, `session_bootstrap()` bundles resume context, health and structural readiness into one follow-up recovery call before broader `memory_context` work begins.
613: 
614: The **commands** are the doors into the system. Each command opens access to the tools it needs. `/speckit:plan --intake-only` owns the standalone intake surface, `/speckit:plan` and `/speckit:complete` reuse the shared intake contract from [intake_contract.md](./references/workflows/intake_contract.md) when the Step 0 local `folder_state` requires delegation, and downstream callers should consume the returned `start_state` as the canonical intake enum. `/deep:research` anchors research to `spec.md` through [spec_check_protocol.md](../deep-loop-workflows/deep-research/references/protocol/spec_check_protocol.md). `/memory:save` updates packet continuity. `/speckit:resume` recovers or continues a previous session.
615: 

(Output capped at 50 KB. Showing lines 1-615. Use offset=616 to continue.)
